title: Chapter 2: 0.7 Hertz
content: The file was 4.3 megabytes. A nothing. A grain of sand. Maya hovered her cursor over the send button and thought about all the ways this could go wrong — the file could be intercepted, NullPointer could be a catfish, a cop, a creep, or worse, someone from Aeterna itself, fishing for whoever was stupid enough to look too closely. Then she thought about Lucy's voicemail, the one she'd played forty-seven times, and the thing that breathed in the background that wasn't Lucy at all.
She hit send.

The upload bar filled and vanished. The Discord message showed a green checkmark. Done. Maya set her phone on the desk, screen up, and waited. The apartment was quiet — the kind of quiet that has texture, that presses against your eardrums like cotton. No sirens tonight. No neighbor's bass through the wall. Just the hum of her laptop fan and the faint electrical whine of the external drive spinning beside it.
One minute. Nothing.
Two minutes. She picked at the edge of a sticker on her audio interface — a cartoon ghost she'd slapped on there two years ago, back when ghosts were funny.
Three minutes of silence, and then her phone lit up. Incoming call. NullPointer.
Maya's thumb hovered. The caller ID showed the Discord icon, a purple smudge. She answered.
A woman's voice — sharp, rapid, no hello — said: "Okay, so you didn't imagine it."
No preamble. No name. Just velocity.

"I ran it through my own spectral analysis and I'm getting a respiratory rate of 0.7 Hertz. That's 42 breaths per minute." A pause so brief it was mechanical, not dramatic. "No human breathes like that. Not even in respiratory distress. Not even infants. Not even dying. Your friend was not alone, and whatever was with her doesn't breathe like us."
Maya's hand went cold around the phone. She switched to speaker and set it down like it was radioactive.
"You're NullPointer," she said.

"I'm Chloe Park. NullPointer is my handle. I don't do mystery for its own sake." Keyboard clicks in the background — fast, deliberate, the rhythm of someone who types the way Maya mixed: by feel, by instinct. "I'm sharing my screen. Accept the invite."

A Discord notification popped up. Maya clicked. Chloe's screen filled her laptop — a dense mosaic of browser tabs, PDF viewers, and a spectral analysis window that made Maya's work look like it had been done on a kitchen table. Which, in fairness, it had.
"The breathing," Maya said. "I isolated it from the voicemail—"

"I know what you did. Your technique isn't bad. You used a noise gate to separate the vocal frequencies from the ambient layer. But you missed the sub-harmonics. You need a narrower bandpass filter." Chloe's cursor moved with surgical precision across the screen, pulling windows forward, stacking data. "I've been tracking Aeterna for seven months. Your audio file is the first hard evidence I've found that connects the disappearances to something physiological."
"Disappearances. Plural."

Chloe didn't answer that. Instead, she maximized a browser tab filled with corporate filings — SEC documents, Delaware business registrations, patent applications. The layout was institutional, hostile to human eyes, and Chloe navigated it like she'd written the index herself.

"Here's what's public. Aeterna Holdings, incorporated 2012, Delaware shell. Subsidiary Aeterna Health, the consumer-facing arm — that's Lumina, the app. Subsidiary Aeterna Therapeutics, the biotech side. Subsidiary Aeterna Facilities — that one's interesting, barely any revenue, mostly real estate holdings." She clicked through to a personnel page. "Key officers. CFO, General Counsel, VP of Engineering — all normal LinkedIn humans with normal digital histories. Midwest colleges, previous jobs at Johnson & Johnson, Salesforce, the usual. Then there's the CEO."

A photo appeared on screen. A man in his mid-forties, though the age was difficult to pin down — the kind of face that could be thirty-five or sixty depending on the light. High cheekbones, dark hair swept back, slate-grey eyes. He wore a dark collarless shirt, almost a tunic, and looked into the camera with the calm curiosity of someone watching an insect.

"Alexander Vlad," Chloe said. "And here's where it gets weird. This guy has no digital footprint before 2011. No college records. No medical records. No social media. Not even a MySpace from 2006. Every other executive at Aeterna has a trail that goes back to their high school debate team. Vlad doesn't exist before he founded the company."
"You can't delete yourself that completely," Maya said. "Not from everything."

"Exactly. You can scrub social media, sure. But university records, property records, voter registration — those are archived in ways you'd need institutional access to alter." Chloe's voice dropped half a register. "It's like he didn't exist before he founded Aeterna. Or he's very, very old and very, very careful."

Maya's mouth went dry. The rational part of her brain — the part that had spent four years in audio engineering school learning about Nyquist frequencies and phase cancellation — wanted to dismiss that. Old. Careful. Those were just metaphors for powerful, connected, rich. But the other part, the part that had listened to Lucy's voicemail forty-seven times, the part that had heard the breathing and known in her guts before her brain caught up — that part heard "very, very old" and thought: you mean it literally.
"Show me the spectral analysis again," Maya said. "The breathing."

Chloe pulled it up. The waveform filled the screen — a complex, undulating shape with sharp peaks where Lucy's voice had been and a lower, more regular pattern underneath. The breathing. Maya had isolated it once already, but Chloe's tools had stripped it cleaner, revealed more detail. The 0.7Hz rhythm was unmistakable now, a steady pulse like a bioelectric metronome.

But Maya was looking at something else. Between the breaths, in the micro-silences, there was a faint secondary pattern — not a sound exactly, more like a shadow of a sound. A sub-harmonic resonance at around 200Hz, with overtones that didn't match open-air recording. They matched reflection. Specific reflection.
"Can you boost the frequencies between 150 and 300Hz?" Maya asked.

Chloe didn't question it. She adjusted the EQ in real time, and the waveform shifted. The breathing faded to a muted throb. What emerged was a ghostly architecture of echoes — tightly spaced, overlapping, with a decay time that spoke of hard surfaces and trapped air.

Maya leaned closer to her laptop screen. The waveform told her what her ears couldn't yet parse: the sound was bouncing off concrete walls, maybe twelve feet apart. Low ceiling — the early reflections came back too fast for anything else. No diffraction pattern to suggest windows. No ambient leak for doorways.
"Lucy was underground when she recorded this," Maya whispered.
The words came out before she could shape them, and they hung in the air of her apartment like smoke.

Chloe went quiet. Ten seconds. Fifteen. When she spoke again, her voice had lost its clinical speed. It was measured now, careful. "Aeterna's campus in Palo Alto — I found the building permits last month. Three sublevels not on any public plan. Two are listed as 'server infrastructure.' Reasonable. Every big tech company has underground data centers."
"And the third?"
"Doesn't have a description. Just a classification." A pause. "'Restricted — Executive Access Only.'"
Maya closed her eyes. She could see it — concrete walls, low ceiling, no windows. A space designed to contain. Not data. Something else.
"Send me the permits," she said.
"Already in your inbox. Along with something else."
A link appeared in the Discord chat. Chloe said: "Lumina's privacy policy. Read it with me."

Maya clicked. The page loaded — dense, corporate, the kind of document nobody reads and everyone agrees to. But Chloe had annotated it. Margin notes in red, expanding each clause into plain language, and what emerged was not a privacy policy but a blueprint for harvest.

Blood type, pulled from lab integration partnerships with Quest Diagnostics and LabCorp. Genetic markers, shared through 23andMe-style API partnerships buried in subsection 14(c). Menstrual cycle tracking, presented as a "fertility awareness feature." Sleep patterns and REM cycles, harvested from the app's integration with every major wearable. Location history, continuous, even when the app was closed. Heart rate variability. Blood oxygen. Cortisol estimates derived from sleep and HRV data.

"This isn't a health app," Chloe said. Her voice was flat, almost mechanical, as if the only way to say it without shaking was to strip every syllable of emotion. "It's a catalog. They're building a database of women's bodies, down to the cellular level. And 50 million people voluntarily gave them everything."

The number landed on Maya like a weight. Fifty million. She scrolled through Lucy's Instagram in her mind — she'd memorized the grid by now, every post a mosaic tile in a life that had stopped updating twelve days ago. There it was, post after post: the Lumina interface screenshot, the daily wellness score, the ovulation calendar shared as a "fertility journey" with heart emojis. Lucy had been using the app for eight months. She'd posted about it constantly. She'd been proud of her streaks, her data points, her compliance.
Maya's stomach dropped through the floor.

"She gave them everything," Maya said. Not an accusation. A fact. Lucy had walked in with open arms and a heart emoji and handed over the full inventory of her own body to a company with a CEO who didn't exist before 2011 and a sublevel marked "Restricted — Executive Access Only."
"That's the design," Chloe said. "Make it beautiful. Make it shareable. Make it identity. The best database is the one people build for you."
The apartment felt smaller. The ceiling lower. Maya's headphones sat on the desk like a dead thing, the padded ear cups pressed flat against the wood. She touched them — they were cold.
"What do you think happened to her?"

The question had been building since the DM, since the first spectral pass, since the moment she'd isolated the breathing and known — known — that it wasn't human. But saying it out loud was different. Saying it out loud made it a question that required an answer, and answers were dangerous.

Chloe didn't answer immediately. The silence stretched, filled only by the faint hiss of the call connection. When she spoke, her voice was lower, stripped of its analytical speed, and what remained was something raw underneath.
"I think something ancient is running a very modern operation. And I think your friend got selected."
Ancient. There it was again. Not a metaphor this time. Chloe Park, who had spent seven months building a case from corporate filings and building permits, had just said *ancient* like she meant it.
"What makes you think I can do anything about it?" Maya asked.

"Because you heard it. The breathing. Most people wouldn't hear it — their brains would smooth it over, file it under 'background noise.' You didn't. You isolated it. That means your ears work differently than most. And right now, I need someone whose ears work differently."

A file appeared in the chat. Screenshot. Aeterna's careers page, clean and corporate, sans-serif font on white space, the kind of design that cost a quarter million dollars and said nothing. But the text said something:

**Audio Visual Technician — Palo Alto Campus**
*Must be comfortable with restricted access environments.*
*$180,000/year + equity + benefits*

Maya stared at it. A hundred and eighty thousand dollars. More than she'd ever made. More than she could make in five years of freelance mixing and podcast cleanup. And "restricted access environments" — they might as well have written *the sublevel*.
"I'm not suggesting you apply," Chloe said. "I'm telling you that's how we get inside."

Maya looked at the job listing on her screen. She looked at her audio interface, the Focusrite with the cartoon ghost peeling at the corner. She looked at her phone — the lock screen still showing Lucy's last selfie, the one from the Lumina company retreat, Lucy in a branded hoodie, grinning, flush with belonging.
Still no reply. Twelve days. No reply.
"I'm not applying for the job," Maya said. "Not yet."

She reached for her headphones. The pads were cold against her palms, colder than the room should have allowed, like they'd been sitting in a draft that didn't exist. She slid them on. The pressure was familiar, the clasp of the headband, the seal of the cups closing over her ears and shutting out the world.
"But I need to hear that recording again. Every frequency. Every reflection. Every ghost in the signal."
She hit play.
The voicemail filled her skull. Lucy's voice, thin and strained: *Maya, I don't know if this is going through, I'm in a dead zone, I think—* Then the cut. Then the breathing.
0.7 Hertz. Forty-two breaths per minute. Not human. Not human. Not human.

But Maya wasn't listening to the breathing this time. She was listening under it. Past it. Through it. She'd adjusted her EQ before hitting play — a custom curve she'd built over four years of pulling voices out of noise. She carved out the breathing's fundamental frequency, suppressed the harmonics, opened a window in the noise floor.
And there, beneath the 0.7Hz rhythm, in the narrow band between the respiratory pulse and the concrete echo, she heard it.
A faint clicking sound.

Not random. Not electrical interference. Not the tap of a pipe or the tick of a cooling server. Rhythmic. Deliberate. A pattern — short, short, long, short — repeated, then shifted, then repeated again with variation. Like someone tapping out a message against a surface. Against a wall.
Against a concrete wall in an underground room with no windows.
Maya's eyes widened.

The clicking continued — faint, buried, almost erased by time and compression and the sheer wrongness of the breathing that covered it. But it was there. It had always been there. She'd played the voicemail forty-seven times and missed it because the breathing was so loud, so *wrong*, that it consumed all available attention.
Lucy was trying to communicate.

Maya's finger hovered over the spacebar. The recording kept playing. The clicking kept tapping. And somewhere beneath the 42-breaths-per-minute horror of whatever shared that room with her best friend, Lucy Chen was knocking on the walls of her own prison.
Maya didn't stop the recording. She reached for a notepad instead.
The clicking continued. Short, short, long, short.
Maya started counting.
